summary: Software Engineer at MathWorks with 6+ years of professional experience spanning game development, full-stack engineering, and large-scale desktop tooling. Proven track record across AAA game studios, SaaS startups, and enterprise software — building systems in C++, MATLAB, and JavaScript. Strong foundation in low-level design, cross-team platform engineering, and shipping production software used by engineers worldwide.

responsibilities: Architected and shipped a data-driven Wizard Framework adopted by 5–6 engineering teams across ~8 products — see Projects for full design detail

Built and maintained frontend and backend components for Simulink configuration management in C++, MATLAB, and JavaScript
Collaborated across product teams to define shared platform conventions and onboarding patterns for the wizard infrastructure
achievements: Designed the Wizard Framework from scratch — replaced a predecessor system with cyclic JSON references, cutting per-wizard definition size and eliminating a class of structural runtime errors
Delivered v1 of the framework in ~6 weeks as sole architect and developer, now in production use across multiple MathWorks products
Improved test coverage and reliability of Simulink.ConfigSet configuration management pipeline

responsibilities: Rolled out major visualization beta features into production to improve chart loading performance
Re-engineered the error boundary system in React.js to specify chart loading error details
Worked on maintenance of existing features in backend
Added unit tests and snapshot tests in Cypress and Jest for chart visualization features
achievements: Enhanced error handling and user experience
Increased test coverage for visualization features

responsibilities: Worked on High Speed CT scanning - Aviation security product, installed at 1200+ Airport security checkpoints
Participated in backend tasks in Java and MySQL including data migration and database partitioning
Created a logging module in C++ to track UI actions and participated in code reviews
Enhanced existing UI system to match modern UI design trends
achievements: Deployed at 1200+ airport security checkpoints worldwide
Decreased database load by 5% through optimization
Received strong positive client feedback for UI improvements

responsibilities: Worked as AI Programmer implementing, debugging and maintaining AI systems for Prince of Persia
Built Fight States History System for combat move tracking and achievements
Developed Restriction Zones for NPC AI patrol and combat systems
Implemented Fight Achievements Tracking system for player statistics
Created AI fight targeting algorithm using Round robin distribution
achievements: Built multiple core AI systems for AAA game development
Contributed to Prince of Persia game development

responsibilities: Ported Starlink: Battle for Atlas to PC platform using Snowdrop engine
Developed keyboard and mouse input system for Windows platforms
Ported UI screens to work with Keyboard/Mouse inputs without interfering with Controller inputs
Implemented UI scaling for different screen resolutions and dynamic button prompt system
achievements: Successfully ported console game to PC platform
Led and trained newly hired junior programmers
Collaborated effectively with Ubisoft Toronto as lead studio

responsibilities: Collaborated with Ubisoft Montreal, Toronto & Kiev for Far Cry 5 development
Developed Arcade Player Progression UI system showing XP, levels, and rewards

projects.name: Data-Driven Wizard Framework
projects.tagline: A declarative, JSON-configured wizard engine shared across multiple engineering teams
projects.description: Designed and built a reusable multi-step wizard framework from scratch, allowing engineering teams to define configuration workflows declaratively in JSON. The framework manages all UI chrome (breadcrumb, button panel, header, content area) and exposes a lifecycle callback system to a MATLAB backend — keeping business logic completely out of the frontend.

impact.note: Replaced a predecessor system with cyclic JSON references and excessive verbosity, significantly reducing per-wizard definition size while eliminating a class of structural runtime errors

architecture.overview: Backend-driven architecture where a MATLAB layer owns and maintains view models for every rendered option. Teams author wizard definitions in JSON; the framework owns all chrome and routes UI lifecycle events to MATLAB callbacks through an internal communication layer. The JavaScript frontend is a stateless renderer — it applies view model updates from MATLAB to the DOM without holding any business logic.
layers: JSON Schema Layer — declarative step and option definitions, supporting nested panel composition
MATLAB Orchestration Layer — view models, step lifecycle callbacks (onEntry, onValidate, onUpdate, onExit, onComplete), shared context object
Communication Layer — structured bidirectional bridge between the MATLAB backend and JavaScript frontend; all lifecycle events and view model updates flow through here
JavaScript Rendering Layer — stateless renderer that applies incoming view model diffs; exposes a DOM control API (setVisible, setEnabled, setValue) callable from MATLAB
patterns: Composite — nested panel containers in JSON form a UI node tree, allowing teams to declare arbitrary layout depth without custom code
State Machine — wizard steps are states with guarded transitions; onValidate must pass before the framework advances, preventing invalid state progression
Observer — MATLAB publishes view model updates asynchronously; the JS layer subscribes and patches only what changed, avoiding full re-renders
View Model — MATLAB holds the single source of truth for UI state; the JS layer is a pure projection, making the frontend trivially replaceable
Strategy — each step supplies its own callback implementations without modifying the wizard orchestrator, enabling independent team iteration
Template Method — the wizard lifecycle sequence (load → onEntry → render → onValidate → onExit → advance) is fixed by the framework; steps only override the hooks they need

keyFeatures: Declarative JSON step definitions with nested panel/container composition (Composite pattern)
Per-step lifecycle hooks — onEntry, onValidate, onUpdate, onExit, onComplete
Shared context object persisting state across all steps for the full wizard session
Backend DOM control API callable from MATLAB — wizard.setVisible(), wizard.setEnabled(), wizard.setValue()
Breadcrumb, button panel, header, and content area owned entirely by the framework
Schema validation at definition load time — structural errors surfaced before any step renders
benefits: Eliminated duplicated wizard chrome across teams — one framework delivers a consistent UX across ~8 products
Clean separation of concerns: JS owns rendering, MATLAB owns business logic, JSON owns structure — each layer is independently changeable
MATLAB callbacks are pure functions with no UI dependency, making them unit-testable without spinning up a browser or rendering the wizard
All state transitions flow through a single communication layer, making them inspectable and replayable for debugging
New teams onboard by writing JSON and MATLAB callbacks only — zero JavaScript required for standard workflows
Cyclic reference errors of the predecessor system are structurally impossible — the schema enforces a directed acyclic tree at load time

drawbacks.issue: Debugging spans three layers (JSON → MATLAB → JS), which can make root-cause analysis slower than in monolithic implementations
drawbacks.mitigation: Structured logging at each layer boundary; schema validation surfaces config errors before runtime

drawbacks.issue: Message queue round-trips add latency for rapid successive DOM updates (e.g., cascading field visibility changes on a single user action)
drawbacks.mitigation: Batch update support on the MATLAB side allows multiple view model mutations to be published as a single diff, reducing round-trips

drawbacks.issue: The imperative DOM API (setVisible/setEnabled) can become a crutch, producing wizard behavior that bypasses the declarative JSON intent and is harder to reason about
drawbacks.mitigation: Code review guidelines establish declarative-first conventions; imperative API is documented as reserved for genuinely dynamic runtime conditions

drawbacks.issue: The JSON schema grows in complexity over time as teams request new option types, container variants, and edge-case behaviours
drawbacks.mitigation: Versioned schema with backward-compatible extensions; deprecated fields emit warnings at load time rather than breaking silently

improvements: Conditional step branching — skip or dynamically inject steps at runtime based on shared context values, reducing wizard length for users who don't need certain paths
Async onValidate — allow validation callbacks to return a Promise, enabling server-side or compute-heavy validation without blocking the UI thread
Wizard state serialization — persist the shared context object so that interrupted sessions can be resumed from the last completed step
Schema IDE tooling — publish a JSON Schema definition enabling autocomplete and inline validation in editors, reducing authoring errors before runtime
Built-in telemetry hooks — standardised analytics on step completion rates and exit points, giving teams visibility into where users abandon configuration flows
Accessibility layer — ARIA landmark roles, focus management between steps, and keyboard navigation baked into the framework chrome so teams get it automatically

projects.name: Personal Portfolio Website
projects.tagline: A fully data-driven portfolio with automated live music stats
projects.description: Built a configurable React portfolio where all content — work history, skills, music events — is driven by JSON files with zero hardcoded UI copy. Integrated automated Stats.fm API data fetching via GitHub Actions, keeping live listening statistics updated weekly without exposing API keys to the browser.
features: Fully data-driven — all content managed via JSON, no hardcoded UI text
Automated weekly music stats via GitHub Actions (Stats.fm API, secret key never reaches the browser)
Dark / light theme with localStorage persistence
Music events timeline with concert reviews and ratings
CI/CD pipeline deploying to GitHub Pages on every push
